mappings.rationaleAI: Western astronomy from prehistoric observations through the Copernican revolution directly addresses the history of astronomy across all periods (A.1.a through A.1.e). The emergence of astrophysics connects to the nature and scope of astronomy (B).
Physics from ancient Greece through 20th-century professionalization directly addresses the full history of physics (A.2.a through A.2.e) and the nature and scope of physics (C).
Chemistry from alchemy through the periodic table directly addresses the history of chemistry across all periods (A.3.a through A.3.d).
Overview of physics covering classical mechanics through quantum theory directly addresses the nature and scope of physics (C.1-C.4).
Foundational chemistry covering the periodic table and molecular structure addresses the nature and scope of chemistry (D.1-D.3).
Physical chemistry addresses subdivisions of chemistry (D.2), chemical transformations (D.4), and thermodynamics developments (A.2.d.iii).
Carbon chemistry addresses subdivisions of chemistry (D.2) and organic chemistry development (A.3.c.v).
Chemistry of living cells addresses biochemistry as a subdivision of chemistry (D.2).
Periodic table history addresses its development (A.3.c.i), element discoveries (A.3.c.ii), and the nature of chemistry (D.1).
Chemical elements address periodic table development (A.3.c.i), element discovery (A.3.c.ii), and the nature of chemistry (D.1).
Molecular science addresses the nature of chemistry (D.1), subdivisions (D.2), and molecular structure theories (A.3.c.iv).
Isotopes relate to discoveries of radioactive elements (A.3.c.ii) and the nature of chemistry (D.1).
Radioactivity relates to the discovery of radioactivity in physics (A.2.d.iv) and radioactive elements in chemistry (A.3.c.ii).
Big Bang theory and cosmic microwave background address relativistic cosmologies (A.1.f.iv), cosmic background radiation (A.1.f.vii), and astrophysics (B.5).
Physics of stars and galaxies addresses 20th-century astronomy (A.1.f), stellar evolution theories (A.1.f.ii), and astrophysics (B.5).
Stellar physics addresses stellar structure theories (A.1.f.ii) and astrophysics (B.5).
Galactic classification addresses statistical studies of galaxies (A.1.f.i), relativistic cosmologies (A.1.f.iv), and astrophysics (B.5).
Black hole physics addresses possible black holes (A.1.f.vii), general relativity tests (A.1.f.iii), and astrophysics (B.5).
Planetary science addresses solar-system astronomy (A.1.f.v) and planetary sciences (B.2).
Natural satellites address solar-system astronomy (A.1.f.v) and planetary sciences (B.2).
Solar physics relates to astrophysics (B.5).
Planetary system formation addresses solar-system astronomy (A.1.f.v) and component disciplines of astronomy (B.2).
Telescope design addresses the invention of the telescope (A.1.c.iii), multi-wavelength astronomy (A.1.f.vi), and instrumentation advances (A.1.f.viii).
Relativity addresses astronomical tests of general relativity (A.1.f.iii), development of relativity (A.2.e.i), and component disciplines of physics (C.2).
Quantum theory addresses quantum theory development (A.2.e.ii), component disciplines (C.2), and philosophical problems in physics (C.6).
Quarks and the Higgs boson address elementary particles (A.2.e.iii) and component disciplines (C.2).
Atomic structure and radioactivity address radioactivity discovery (A.2.d.iv), nuclear interactions (A.2.e.iii), and component disciplines (C.2).
The four laws of thermodynamics address thermodynamics developments (A.2.d.iii) and component disciplines (C.2).
Electromagnetic theory addresses electricity and magnetism development (A.2.d.ii) and component disciplines (C.2).
Physics of light addresses the wave vs corpuscular theory debate (A.2.d.i) and component disciplines (C.2).
Physics of sound relates to component disciplines of physics (C.2).
Wave phenomena address theories of light (A.2.d.i) and component disciplines (C.2).
Superconductors and superfluids address condensed-matter physics (A.2.e.iv) and component disciplines (C.2).
Superconductivity addresses condensed-matter physics developments (A.2.e.iv) and component disciplines (C.2).
Physics of matter addresses the nature of physics (C.1) and component disciplines (C.2).
Gravitational theory addresses universal gravitation (A.1.c.iv), relativity development (A.2.e.i), and component disciplines (C.2).
X-ray diffraction addresses methods of chemical analysis (A.3.c.iii) and methods of chemistry (D.3).
Physical and chemical properties of water address the nature of chemistry (D.1).
Galileo's contributions address 16th-17th century astronomy (A.1.c) and physics (A.2.c).
Newton's mechanics address universal gravitation (A.1.c.iv), 16th-17th century physics (A.2.c), and mechanical philosophy's influence on chemistry (A.3.a.ii).
The heliocentric revolution addresses geocentric and heliocentric world systems (A.1.c.i).
Electromagnetic induction addresses electricity and magnetism development (A.2.d.ii).
Bohr's contributions address quantum theory development (A.2.e.ii) and atomic structure theories (A.2.e.iii).
Geophysics addresses interdisciplinary fields of physics (C.5).
Fluid mechanics relates to component disciplines of physics (C.2).
Polymers and liquid crystals relate to condensed-matter physics (A.2.e.iv) and component disciplines (C.2).
Materials science relates to physics and other disciplines (C.4) and subdivisions of chemistry (D.2).
Entropy and thermodynamics address thermodynamics developments (A.2.d.iii) and component disciplines (C.2).
Philosophical foundations of physics address philosophical problems in physics (C.6).
Nanoscale science relates to modern developments in physics (A.2.e.v) and physics and other disciplines (C.4).
Integration of astronomy and planetary science relates to component disciplines of astronomy (B.2) and interdisciplinary physics (C.5).
Vacuum physics and quantum field theory relate to quantum theory development (A.2.e.ii) and philosophical problems (C.6).
Nuclear power relates to nuclear interactions theories (A.2.e.iii).
Nuclear weapons relate to nuclear interactions theories (A.2.e.iii).
Mineralogy relates to subdivisions of chemistry (D.2) and methods of chemistry (D.3).
Chemistry of combustion relates to chemical transformations (D.4).
